refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) over the evaluation period, and that these data are accessible to stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance) for calculation and reporting of energy_per_ton. Confirm the measurement system is calibrated and traceable.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent unit) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall record and store energy and work output data with sufficient temporal resolution to enable post-operation analysis.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide stakeholders access to energy and work output data for calculation and reporting of energy efficiency metrics.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period

DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation
DP3: Decompose energy consumption into sub-processes (transport, loading, unloading); assign work output proportionally to each; compute energy_per_ton per process and aggregate
DP4: Establish energy baseline from historical operational data; measure incremental energy consumption and work output during evaluation period; compute delta energy_per_ton relative to baseline
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation

potential_sources: Sensor calibration drift across heterogeneous equipment types may require independent per-unit validation protocols (mitigated by local aggregation layer)
Central analytics engine creates single point of dependency for metric computation; recommend fallback local computation capability
Real-time streaming introduces timing synchronization requirement between energy and tonnage measurements; must define acceptable skew tolerance
coupling_check.notes: DP2 exhibits low functional coupling: energy measurement is logically independent of tonnage measurement; aggregation and computation are decoupled from data collection. Temporal coupling (synchronization) is manageable through explicit interface specification. DP1 is simpler but introduces batch-processing latency and loses visibility into operational efficiency during evaluation period. DP3 adds unnecessary decomposition complexity without improving acceptance criterion satisfaction. DP4 introduces baseline dependency risk if historical data is unreliable or operational context shifts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is metered at a single point (fuel tank, electrical panel, or equivalent). RISK: Distributed or multi-source energy inputs (e.g., multiple fuel depots, grid + generator hybrid) require extended instrumentation scope.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) is measurable at delivery point with acceptable accuracy. RISK: Partial loads, spillage, or measurement ambiguity (e.g., 'moved' vs. 'delivered') must be defined operationally.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is continuous and uninterrupted. RISK: Operational downtime, maintenance windows, or weather stoppages must be explicitly included/excluded from energy_per_ton calculation.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor accuracy and calibration are sufficient to detect variance within E_target margin. RISK: If E_target margin is <5%, sensor uncertainty may dominate; recommend uncertainty budget analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit real-time sensor deployment or data streaming. RISK: Worksite restrictions (e.g., no wireless, air-gapped networks) may force DP1 or DP4 instead.

ASSUMPTION: Central analytics engine is accessible and reliable during evaluation period. RISK: Network outages or system unavailability could invalidate continuous reporting; recommend local fallback computation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR shall be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'energy consumed' (e.g., fuel burned, electrical energy drawn, or net energy after parasitic losses); (2) Definition of 'unit of work output' (e.g., tonnage delivered to final destination, tonnage loaded, or tonnage moved any distance); (3) Acceptable measurement uncertainty and sensor accuracy requirements; (4) Handling of non-productive energy (idle, setup, maintenance) during evaluation period; (5) Frequency and format of reporting (real-time dashboard, periodic summary, or both). These refinements will constrain DP selection and enable rigorous acceptance testing. Additionally, confirm that DP2's real-time streaming architecture is compatible with worksite safety and operational policies; if not, escalate to DP1 (batch post-evaluation) or DP4 (baseline delta) as fallback.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized real-time energy and tonnage monitoring system encompassing sensor integration, local aggregation logic, streaming pipeline, analytics engine, and continuous KPI computation (energy_per_ton)

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: raw energy and tonnage sensor streams from Vehicle; Outbound: normalized metrics and energy_per_ton KPIs to central analytics and reporting systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context (mission parameters, load specifications) to calibrate sensor normalization and validation thresholds

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Triggers alerts if energy_per_ton deviates beyond safe operational bounds during periodic validation

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides reliable transport for sensor data streaming and receipt of configuration/validation parameters from remote analytics

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy and tonnage analytics. Sensor embedding and local aggregation are Vehicle responsibilities (minor diffusion), but all streaming, normalization, and central analytics logic remain within NGHE. Periodic validation and KPI reporting are core NGHE functions. Minimal external diffusion via Telemetry and Network Link interfaces.

Poor fit: Vehicle would only contain physical sensor hardware and local aggregation. The bulk of DP logic—streaming pipeline, normalization, central analytics engine, continuous computation, and validation—would diffuse to NGHE Solution. Creates tight coupling and splits a cohesive analytics function across two modules.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator-centric and command-focused. Energy and tonnage analytics are mission-critical operational metrics, not teleoperation control logic. Placing DP here would cause severe diffusion of analytics logic across NGHE Solution and Vehicle, violating separation of concerns.

Weak fit: Safety Services would naturally consume energy_per_ton validation alerts (SafetyAlert interface), but should not own the analytics engine itself. Placing the entire DP here conflates safety monitoring with energy analytics, diffusing core NGHE operational intelligence and creating inappropriate coupling.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of real-time energy and tonnage analytics. It receives raw sensor telemetry from Vehicle, performs all normalization, aggregation, and KPI computation internally, and exports validated metrics via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. This preserves cohesion of the analytics function and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Vehicle must reliably stream raw sensor data to NGHE Solution; any latency or packet loss in the Telemetry interface degrades real-time computation accuracy.
NGHE Solution depends on accurate sensor calibration from Vehicle; miscalibrated sensors will propagate through the entire analytics pipeline.
Periodic validation logic must be robust; if validation thresholds are misconfigured (via Tasking), false alerts may trigger SafetyAlert, creating operational disruption.
Network Link reliability is critical; if the streaming connection fails, NGHE Solution cannot compute energy_per_ton, creating a single point of failure for mission-critical KPI reporting.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution architecture: real-time sensor embedding + local aggregation + streaming pipeline + central analytics engine + continuous KPI computation. The DP explicitly names the output metric (energy_per_ton) and includes validation cycles, which map cleanly to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The logic is sound and implementable.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Sensor hardware and local aggregation logic reside in Vehicle module, while normalization, streaming, and central analytics remain in NGHE Solution. This split is acceptable (containment_score 0.92 for NGHE) but creates a dependency on Vehicle's reliable telemetry stream; any sensor calibration drift or packet loss in the Telemetry interface will degrade analytics accuracy.

Network Link reliability is a single point of failure: if the streaming connection between Vehicle and NGHE Solution fails, energy_per_ton computation halts entirely. No fallback or local caching strategy is specified in the DP.

Validation threshold configuration (via Tasking interface) is externally controlled; misconfiguration could trigger spurious SafetyAlert outputs, creating operational disruption. No internal validation of validation parameters is mentioned.

Periodic validation logic is mentioned but not detailed; unclear whether validation runs synchronously with continuous computation or asynchronously, and how conflicts are resolved if real-time and periodic results diverge.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct and natural owner of the analytics engine (0.92 containment score). The DP is well-scoped to a single module boundary, avoiding the severe diffusion that would occur if analytics logic were split across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly defined and minimal. The module boundary is architecturally sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly computable from the DP outputs. NGHE Solution will continuously report energy_per_ton; comparison against E_target is straightforward. Periodic validation provides checkpoints. Worksite safety plan integration is addressed via SafetyAlert interface. Measurability is clear.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the analytics engine and KPI computation. Vehicle owns sensor hardware and local aggregation. Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces define responsibility boundaries. Ownership is unambiguous and avoids duplication. However, the DP does not specify fallback behavior if Network Link fails or if Tasking parameters are invalid, creating minor allocation ambiguity in edge cases.

recommended_revisions: Specify Network Link redundancy or local caching strategy: if the streaming connection fails, NGHE Solution should either buffer data locally or degrade gracefully (e.g., report last-known energy_per_ton with a staleness flag) rather than halting computation entirely.

Add validation of Tasking parameters: NGHE Solution should verify that validation thresholds received via Tasking are within acceptable bounds before applying them; reject invalid configurations and log warnings.

Clarify periodic validation timing and conflict resolution: specify whether periodic validation runs asynchronously, how often it executes, and what happens if periodic and continuous energy_per_ton values diverge beyond a tolerance. Define escalation logic.

Document sensor calibration handshake: specify how Vehicle communicates sensor calibration metadata (gain, offset, drift estimates) to NGHE Solution, and how NGHE Solution applies these corrections in the normalization step. This reduces propagation of calibration errors.

Add data quality metrics to Telemetry interface: Vehicle should stream not only raw sensor values but also confidence/quality indicators (e.g., sensor health status, packet loss rate) so NGHE Solution can flag degraded data quality in KPI reports.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary is architecturally correct, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results identify four concrete diffusion risks and edge-case allocation ambiguities that are safety-critical (high safety criticality factor): (1) Network Link single point of failure with no fallback, (2) unvalidated Tasking parameter injection, (3) unclear periodic validation timing and conflict resolution, and (4) missing sensor calibration metadata propagation. These are not showstoppers but represent incomplete specification of the DP that will manifest as implementation risks if not addressed at the next decomposition level. Proceeding to sub-FR derivation allows these concerns to be systematically decomposed into lower-level requirements (e.g., FR for Network Resilience, FR for Parameter Validation, FR for Calibration Handshake) where they can be designed and verified in detail. This approach maintains forward momentum while ensuring safety-critical edge cases are not deferred indefinitely.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Ensure continuous energy_per_ton computation despite transient Network Link failures (resilience requirement; addresses single point of failure risk).

FR2.2: Validate Tasking interface parameters (validation thresholds, evaluation period, KPI targets) before applying them to analytics logic (safety requirement; prevents misconfiguration-induced SafetyAlert spam).

FR2.3: Propagate sensor calibration metadata (gain, offset, drift estimates) from Vehicle to NGHE Solution and apply corrections in normalization step (data quality requirement; reduces calibration error diffusion).

FR2.4: Coordinate periodic validation with continuous energy_per_ton computation and resolve conflicts if results diverge beyond tolerance (timing and consistency requirement; clarifies validation semantics).

FR2.5: Stream data quality indicators (sensor health, packet loss, confidence scores) alongside raw telemetry to enable NGHE Solution to flag degraded data quality in KPI reports (observability requirement; supports trustworthiness of energy_per_ton metric).

revise.why: No revision of current-level FR, DP, or IM is necessary. The gates pass and the recommended revisions are best addressed through sub-FR decomposition rather than rework at this level. Revising the DP now would risk scope creep and obscure the architectural clarity already achieved. Sub-FRs will inherit the parent DP's core logic while adding specificity to edge cases.

assumptions: Network Link between Vehicle and NGHE Solution is available for the majority of the evaluation period; transient failures are tolerable if fallback/caching is implemented.
Tasking interface is trusted to deliver parameters in good faith, but validation is still required as a defensive measure (defense-in-depth).
Sensor calibration metadata is available from Vehicle (either pre-loaded or streamed dynamically); NGHE Solution has the computational capacity to apply corrections in real-time.

Periodic validation is a secondary check and does not override continuous computation; conflicts are resolved by escalation to human operators or automated fallback logic (to be specified in sub-FRs).
Data quality indicators can be computed by Vehicle with minimal overhead and streamed alongside telemetry without exceeding bandwidth constraints.
Safety criticality of energy_per_ton metric justifies the additional complexity of resilience, validation, and quality monitoring; the cost-benefit is favorable.
open_risks: Network Link redundancy strategy not yet chosen (e.g., dual connectivity, local buffering, graceful degradation); sub-FR2.1 must evaluate trade-offs.
Tasking parameter validation logic not yet specified; unclear whether validation is synchronous (reject invalid params immediately) or asynchronous (log and alert); sub-FR2.2 must define semantics.
Periodic validation frequency and tolerance thresholds not yet defined; sub-FR2.4 must establish timing and conflict resolution rules to avoid operational confusion.
Sensor calibration handshake protocol not yet designed; unclear whether calibration metadata is static (pre-loaded) or dynamic (streamed); sub-FR2.3 must specify the protocol.

Data quality metrics not yet enumerated; sub-FR2.5 must define which indicators are most critical (e.g., sensor health vs. packet loss vs. confidence score) and how they influence KPI trustworthiness.

Integration with worksite safety plan (via SafetyAlert interface) is mentioned but not detailed; unclear how SafetyAlert outputs are consumed by safety systems and whether there are feedback loops; may require additional sub-FR if safety integration is complex.
evidence_needed: Specification of Network Link SLA (availability, latency, packet loss tolerance) and evaluation of whether fallback/caching is necessary to meet energy_per_ton reporting SLA.
Definition of valid ranges for Tasking parameters (validation thresholds, evaluation period, KPI targets) and validation logic (e.g., range checks, consistency checks).
Specification of sensor calibration metadata format, update frequency, and application method in normalization step; validation that calibration corrections do not introduce bias or instability.
Timing specification for periodic validation (frequency, duration, synchronization with continuous computation) and conflict resolution rules (e.g., tolerance band, escalation logic).
Enumeration of data quality indicators and their impact on energy_per_ton trustworthiness; definition of thresholds for flagging degraded data quality.
Integration specification for SafetyAlert interface: how alerts are generated, routed, and consumed by safety systems; feedback loops and escalation paths.
Acceptance test plan for each sub-FR, including edge cases (Network Link failure, invalid Tasking params, sensor calibration drift, periodic validation conflicts, data quality degradation).
